Design a recursive function to clear the k-th bit (0-indexed) of an integer n. Use only bitwise operations and explain the recursive bit manipulation.
Implement a recursive algorithm to toggle the k-th bit (0-indexed) of an integer n. Describe the recursive toggling mechanism.
Write a recursive function to check if the k-th bit (0-indexed) of an integer n is set. Use only bitwise operations and explain the recursive checking process.
Given two integers, recursively compute their sum using only bitwise operations (AND, XOR, left shift). Explain the recursive addition of carry and sum.
Create a recursive function to find the position of the rightmost set bit in an integer. Use only bitwise operations and detail the recursive search.
Implement a recursive algorithm to count the number of bits that need to be flipped to convert integer A to integer B. Explain the recursive comparison of bits.
Write a recursive function to find the maximum XOR value for two numbers in an array. Use only bitwise operations and describe the recursive maximization process.
Given an integer n, recursively generate the gray code sequence for n bits. Explain the recursive construction of gray codes.
Implement a recursive function to find the missing number in an array of size n containing numbers from 0 to n. Use only bitwise XOR and explain the recursive accumulation.
Write a recursive algorithm to compute the bitwise OR of all subsets of an array. Describe the recursive subset generation and OR accumulation.
Create a recursive function to determine if an integer has alternating bits (e.g., 101010). Use only bitwise operations and explain the recursive bit comparison.
Given an array where every number appears three times except one which appears once, find the unique number using bitwise operations recursively. Explain the recursive bit counting process.

Given a network address 192.168.0.0/16, design a subnetting scheme that accommodates 5 departments with the following requirements: Dept A needs 120 hosts, Dept B needs 60 hosts, Dept C needs 30 hosts, Dept D needs 15 hosts, and Dept E needs 7 hosts. Determine the subnet mask, network address, broadcast address, and usable host range for each department, while minimizing wasted IP addresses. Provide the solution in CIDR notation.

A company has been allocated the network 172.16.0.0/21. They need to create subnets for 8 branches, each requiring between 90 and 100 hosts. Determine if this is possible with the given address space. If possible, provide the subnet details for each branch. If not, propose the smallest supernet that would accommodate all branches and recalculate.

You are given the IP address 10.10.10.10/27. Determine all possible subnetworks that this address could belong to if the network was subnetted from a /24 prefix. List each subnet's network address, broadcast address, and usable range, then identify which subnet contains the given IP.

Design a Variable Length Subnet Mask (VLSM) scheme for the network 192.168.100.0/24 to support: 3 LANs with 50 hosts each, 2 LANs with 25 hosts each, and 4 point-to-point links. Provide the subnet mask, network address, and usable IP range for each segment, ensuring no overlap and efficient address utilization.

A network administrator needs to connect 12 regional offices, each requiring a subnet that supports at least 500 hosts. The provider has allocated 10.0.0.0/8. Determine the optimal subnet mask for each office, calculate the first three subnets' network addresses, broadcast addresses, and usable host ranges, and specify how many such subnets can be created from the /8 block.

Given the network 198.51.100.0/24, subnet it to create 14 subnets with the maximum possible number of hosts per subnet. Determine the new subnet mask, the number of hosts per subnet, and list the network address, broadcast address, and usable IP range for the 5th and 10th subnets.

You have been assigned the IPv6 prefix 2001:db8:acad::/48. Design a subnetting plan to allocate /64 subnets to 16 campuses, each campus further subnetted into 8 buildings with /72 subnets, and each building having 256 rooms with /80 subnets. Calculate the total number of subnets at each level and provide the network prefix for the 3rd room in the 5th building of the 2nd campus.

A company uses the network 172.30.0.0/16. They want to create subnets for 60 departments, each needing exactly 254 usable hosts. Determine if this is feasible. If yes, provide the subnet mask and the network address of the 30th department. If not, suggest an alternative plan using VLSM to meet the requirement with minimal waste.

Given the IP address 203.0.113.67 with subnet mask 255.255.255.240, determine the network address, broadcast address, number of usable hosts, and which subnet number this address belongs to if the parent network is 203.0.113.0/24. Also, calculate the subnet mask in CIDR notation.

Design a hierarchical subnetting scheme for a university with the network 10.0.0.0/14. The university has 4 colleges, each college has 8 departments, and each department needs 120 hosts. Allocate addresses efficiently, providing the subnet mask and network address for each college and for the 3rd department in the 2nd college.

You are given two networks: 192.168.1.0/25 and 192.168.1.128/26. Determine if these networks can be summarized into a single route advertisement. If yes, provide the summary network address and mask. If not, explain why and suggest the smallest possible summary that includes both.

A network 169.254.0.0/16 must be subnetted to support 20 subnets with the maximum possible hosts. After subnetting, you need to further divide the 5th subnet into 4 equal-sized subnets. Provide the subnet details for the original 20 subnets and the further divided subnets from the 5th, including masks and usable ranges.

Given the network address 198.162.0.0/22, calculate the subnet mask that allows for 10 subnets, each with at least 100 hosts. Determine the network address, broadcast address, and usable host range for the 7th subnet, and verify that it meets the host requirement.

An ISP allocates 220.100.80.0/21 to a business. The business needs to create subnets for 6 offices: Office1 (200 hosts), Office2 (100 hosts), Office3 (50 hosts), Office4 (25 hosts), Office5 (10 hosts), Office6 (5 hosts). Use VLSM to allocate addresses efficiently. Provide each office's subnet mask, network address, and usable IP range.

You have the IP address 172.31.255.200/23. Determine the network address, broadcast address, and total number of addresses in the subnet. Then, if this subnet is further divided into 8 equal subnets, provide the network address and broadcast address of the 6th smaller subnet.

Design a subnetting plan for a company with network 10.10.0.0/20 to accommodate: 1 subnet with 1000 hosts, 3 subnets with 500 hosts each, and 5 subnets with 250 hosts each. Use VLSM to minimize waste. Provide each subnet's mask, network address, and usable host range.

Given the network 192.0.2.0/28, list all possible subnet addresses, their broadcast addresses, and usable host ranges. Then, determine which subnet the IP 192.0.2.30 belongs to, and calculate the subnet mask in dotted decimal and CIDR notation.

A global organization has the IPv6 block 2001:0db8:1234::/36. They need to allocate /48 blocks to 64 regional offices, each regional office allocates /56 blocks to 16 branches, and each branch allocates /64 blocks to 256 departments. Calculate the total number of departments supported and provide the network prefix for the 12th department in the 8th branch of the 20th regional office.

You are given the network 172.16.0.0/19. Subnet it to create 12 subnets, each with an equal number of hosts. Determine the new subnet mask, the number of usable hosts per subnet, and provide the network address, broadcast address, and usable range for the 9th subnet. Then, further subnet the 9th subnet into 4 equal subnets and provide details for the 3rd of these.

A company uses network 10.0.0.0/8. They want to create a subnetting scheme where each subnet supports between 4000 and 5000 hosts. Determine the optimal subnet mask, the number of such subnets possible, and provide the network address, broadcast address, and usable range for the 15th subnet. Also, calculate the subnet mask in dotted decimal format.
Balance the combustion reaction of butane (C4H10) with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water, ensuring all coefficients are integers.
Balance the reaction between aluminum and sulfuric acid to produce aluminum sulfate and hydrogen gas, then determine the smallest integer coefficients.
Balance the decomposition of ammonium nitrate into nitrogen gas, oxygen gas, and water vapor, requiring recursive adjustment of nitrogen and oxygen atoms.
Balance the reaction of iron(III) oxide with carbon monoxide to produce iron and carbon dioxide, using a systematic approach to handle the transition metal.
Balance the synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen gases, ensuring the stoichiometry reflects the diatomic nature of the reactants.
Balance the reaction between potassium permanganate and hydrochloric acid to produce potassium chloride, manganese(II) chloride, chlorine gas, and water, which involves multiple redox changes.
Balance the combustion of ethanol (C2H5OH) in oxygen to yield carbon dioxide and water, then verify the oxygen balance recursively.
Balance the reaction of copper with concentrated nitric acid to produce copper(II) nitrate, nitrogen dioxide, and water, accounting for the oxidation state changes.
Balance the decomposition of potassium chlorate into potassium chloride and oxygen gas, using integer coefficients only.
Balance the reaction between sodium hydroxide and phosphoric acid to produce sodium phosphate and water, considering the polyatomic phosphate group.
Balance the oxidation of glucose (C6H12O6) by oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water, ensuring all atoms are conserved in this biochemical equation.
Balance the reaction of calcium carbide with water to produce acetylene gas and calcium hydroxide, then check the hydrogen and oxygen balance recursively.
Balance the thermite reaction between aluminum and iron(III) oxide to produce aluminum oxide and iron metal, handling the metal oxides systematically.
Balance the reaction of sulfur dioxide with oxygen to form sulfur trioxide, a key step in the contact process for sulfuric acid production.
Balance the neutralization of sulfuric acid with potassium hydroxide to yield potassium sulfate and water, ensuring the sulfate ion remains intact.
Balance the reaction of methane with chlorine gas under UV light to produce carbon tetrachloride and hydrogen chloride, accounting for all chlorine atoms.
Balance the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen gas, using catalysts as a context but balancing without them.
Balance the reaction of zinc with silver nitrate to produce zinc nitrate and silver metal, ensuring charge and atom conservation.
Balance the combustion of propane (C3H8) in oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water, then recursively adjust coefficients to minimize them.
Balance the reaction of lead(II) nitrate with potassium iodide to produce lead(II) iodide and potassium nitrate, a double displacement requiring careful handling of polyatomic ions.

A 5.00 g sample of a mixture containing calcium carbonate and magnesium carbonate is heated strongly until no further mass loss occurs. The residue weighs 2.50 g. Calculate the mass percentage of calcium carbonate in the original mixture, assuming the decomposition reactions are: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ and MgCO₃ → MgO + CO₂.

A 10.0 L vessel contains a mixture of methane (CH₄) and ethane (C₂H₆) at 25°C and 1.50 atm total pressure. Complete combustion of this mixture with excess oxygen yields 15.0 g of water. Determine the mole fraction of methane in the original mixture.

A 2.50 g sample of an alloy containing only aluminum and magnesium is reacted with excess hydrochloric acid, producing 2.80 L of hydrogen gas at STP. Calculate the mass of aluminum in the alloy, given the reactions: 2Al + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂ and Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂.

A 1.000 g sample of a compound containing only carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen is burned in excess oxygen, yielding 2.190 g of CO₂ and 0.930 g of H₂O. The molar mass of the compound is determined to be 90.08 g/mol. Determine the molecular formula of the compound.

A 25.0 mL sample of sulfuric acid solution is titrated with 0.150 M sodium hydroxide. The equivalence point is reached after 42.5 mL of NaOH is added. Calculate the molarity of the sulfuric acid solution and the mass of H₂SO₄ present in the original sample.

A mixture of potassium chlorate and potassium chloride weighing 5.00 g is heated until all potassium chlorate decomposes: 2KClO₃ → 2KCl + 3O₂. The mass of the residue after heating is 3.80 g. Calculate the percentage by mass of potassium chlorate in the original mixture.

A 0.500 g sample of a hydrocarbon is burned in excess oxygen, producing 1.55 g of CO₂ and 0.697 g of H₂O. The density of the hydrocarbon vapor at STP is 2.59 g/L. Determine the molecular formula of the hydrocarbon.

A 100.0 mL sample of a solution containing both HCl and H₃PO₄ is titrated with 0.200 M NaOH. The first equivalence point (neutralization of HCl and first proton of H₃PO₄) requires 45.0 mL, and the second equivalence point (neutralization of second proton of H₃PO₄) requires an additional 25.0 mL. Calculate the molarity of HCl and H₃PO₄ in the original solution.

A 5.00 g sample of a mixture of sodium carbonate and sodium bicarbonate is heated strongly. The loss in mass due to decomposition is 0.93 g. Calculate the mass percentage of sodium carbonate in the mixture, given the reactions: 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ and Na₂CO₃ is stable under these conditions.

A 2.00 L flask contains a mixture of nitrogen and oxygen gases at 27°C and 1.20 atm. The mixture is sparked to form nitrogen dioxide (2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂), and after the reaction, the pressure is 0.90 atm at the same temperature. Assuming complete reaction of the limiting reactant, calculate the original mole fractions of nitrogen and oxygen.

A 0.750 g sample of an iron ore is dissolved in acid and reduced to Fe²⁺. Titration with 0.0200 M potassium permanganate requires 25.0 mL to reach the endpoint (MnO₄⁻ + 5Fe²⁺ + 8H⁺ → Mn²⁺ + 5Fe³⁺ + 4H₂O). Calculate the percentage of iron in the ore as Fe₂O₃.

A 10.0 g sample of a mixture containing only calcium oxide and calcium carbonate is treated with excess hydrochloric acid, producing 2.24 L of CO₂ at STP. Calculate the mass of calcium oxide in the mixture, given the reactions: CaO + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O and CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂.

A 1.50 g sample of a compound containing only sodium, carbon, and oxygen is dissolved in water and treated with excess barium chloride, precipitating 1.97 g of barium carbonate. The remaining solution is titrated with 0.100 M HCl, requiring 30.0 mL to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. Determine the formula of the original compound (Na₂CO₃, NaHCO₃, or a mixture).

A 0.250 g sample of a metal carbonate (MCO₃) is dissolved in 50.0 mL of 0.100 M HCl. The excess acid is titrated with 0.0500 M NaOH, requiring 20.0 mL. Calculate the molar mass of the metal and identify the metal if it is in Group 2 of the periodic table.

A 2.00 L vessel at 400 K contains 0.500 atm of PCl₅. After equilibrium is established (PCl₅ ⇌ PCl₃ + Cl₂), the total pressure is 0.700 atm. Calculate the degree of dissociation of PCl₅ and the equilibrium constant Kp.

A 1.000 g sample of a mixture containing only silver and copper is dissolved in nitric acid. The silver is precipitated as AgCl by adding excess NaCl solution, yielding 0.850 g of AgCl. Calculate the mass percentage of silver in the original mixture.

A 0.500 g sample of a compound containing only nitrogen and oxygen is decomposed to produce 0.303 g of nitrogen gas. The density of the compound at STP is 2.05 g/L. Determine the molecular formula of the compound.

A 25.0 mL sample of a solution containing acetic acid and sodium acetate is titrated with 0.100 M NaOH. The initial pH is 4.74, and after adding 12.5 mL of NaOH, the pH is 5.04. Calculate the molarity of acetic acid and sodium acetate in the original solution (pKa of acetic acid = 4.74).

A 5.00 g sample of a mixture containing only Fe and Fe₂O₃ is reacted with excess hydrogen gas at high temperature, reducing all iron to metallic iron. The mass after reduction is 4.20 g. Calculate the mass percentage of Fe in the original mixture.

A 0.200 g sample of a compound containing only carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen is burned in excess oxygen, producing 0.480 g of CO₂, 0.196 g of H₂O, and 0.0560 g of N₂. Determine the empirical formula of the compound.

A car accelerates uniformly from rest at 2 m/s² for 10 seconds, then travels at constant velocity for 20 seconds, and finally decelerates uniformly at 3 m/s² until it stops. What is the total distance traveled by the car?

A ball is thrown vertically upward from the edge of a 50 m high cliff with an initial velocity of 20 m/s. At the same instant, another ball is dropped from the top of the cliff. Determine the time when both balls are at the same height above the ground and what that height is.

A particle moves along a straight line with its position given by x(t) = t³ - 6t² + 9t + 2, where x is in meters and t is in seconds. Find all times when the particle is at rest, and determine the total distance traveled between t = 0 and t = 4 seconds.

Two trains start from stations 240 km apart and travel toward each other. Train A leaves at 8:00 AM with constant speed 60 km/h, while Train B leaves at 8:30 AM with constant speed 80 km/h. At what time do they meet, and how far from Train A's starting point does the meeting occur?

A projectile is launched from ground level at an angle of 30° above the horizontal with an initial speed of 40 m/s. Calculate the time of flight, maximum height reached, and horizontal range. Neglect air resistance.

A car traveling at 25 m/s sees a red light 100 m ahead. The driver applies brakes, causing a constant deceleration of 5 m/s². Determine whether the car stops before the light, and if so, how far from the light it stops. If not, calculate the car's speed as it passes the light.

A particle moves with acceleration a(t) = 2t - 4 m/s². Given that its velocity at t = 0 is 6 m/s and its position at t = 0 is 10 m, find the particle's position when its velocity is zero for the second time.
Two cyclists start from the same point. Cyclist A rides north at 15 km/h, while Cyclist B rides east at 20 km/h. After 2 hours, how fast is the distance between them increasing?

A stone is dropped into a well and the splash is heard 3.5 seconds later. If the speed of sound is 340 m/s, calculate the depth of the well. Consider both the time for the stone to fall and the time for sound to travel back up.

A police car traveling at 30 m/s is chasing a speeding car moving at 25 m/s. Initially, the police car is 100 m behind. If the police car accelerates at 2 m/s², how long will it take to catch the speeding car, assuming the speeding car maintains constant speed?
A particle moves along the x-axis with velocity v(t) = 3t² - 12t + 9. Find the total distance traveled between t = 0 and t = 5 seconds, and determine the average speed over this interval.

A ball is thrown horizontally from the top of a 60 m tall building with a speed of 20 m/s. Calculate the time it takes to hit the ground, its velocity just before impact, and the horizontal distance from the base of the building where it lands.

A train accelerates from rest at a constant rate α for time T₁, then travels at constant velocity for time T₂, and finally decelerates at constant rate β until it stops. If the total distance traveled is D, derive an expression for the maximum velocity reached in terms of α, β, T₁, T₂, and D.

Two projectiles are launched simultaneously from the same point. Projectile A is launched at 30° above horizontal with speed 50 m/s, while Projectile B is launched at 60° above horizontal with speed 40 m/s. Determine if and when they collide, assuming no air resistance.

A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly to a speed of 72 km/h in 20 seconds. It maintains this speed for 2 minutes, then decelerates uniformly to rest in 15 seconds. Calculate the total distance covered and the average speed for the entire journey.
A particle's position is given by r(t) = (3t² - 2t)î + (4t³ - 6t²)ĵ meters. Find the magnitude of acceleration when the particle's speed is minimum.

A hot air balloon is ascending vertically at 5 m/s. When it is 100 m above the ground, a sandbag is released from the balloon. Calculate the time it takes for the sandbag to hit the ground and its velocity upon impact.

Two cars start from the same point. Car A travels east at 40 km/h, while Car B travels north at 30 km/h. After 1 hour, Car A suddenly turns north and continues at 40 km/h, while Car B continues north at 30 km/h. Find the distance between the cars 2 hours after they started.
A particle moves along a parabolic path y = x²/4 (x and y in meters). If the x-component of velocity is constant at 2 m/s, find the magnitude of acceleration when x = 4 m.

A rocket is launched vertically upward with constant acceleration of 20 m/s² for 30 seconds, after which the engines shut off. Calculate the maximum altitude reached and the total time from launch until the rocket hits the ground. Neglect air resistance and assume g = 9.8 m/s².

Find the equivalent resistance between terminals A and B in a circuit consisting of an infinite ladder network where each section contains a 2Ω resistor in series followed by a 3Ω resistor in parallel with the next section. Derive the recurrence relation and solve for Req.

Determine the voltage across the 10Ω resistor in a circuit with a 12V source, where the network consists of a 4Ω resistor in series with a parallel combination of a 6Ω resistor and a series combination of two 8Ω resistors, which is then in parallel with another 8Ω resistor before connecting to the 10Ω load.

Calculate the current through the 5Ω resistor in a circuit with a 20V source, a 10Ω resistor in series with a parallel network of a 15Ω resistor and a series combination of two 5Ω resistors, followed by another 5Ω resistor in parallel with a 10Ω resistor.

Find the Thevenin equivalent circuit between terminals A and B for a network containing a 12V voltage source in series with a 4Ω resistor, connected to a node that splits into a 6Ω resistor to ground and a 3Ω resistor leading to another node with a 2A current source pointing into it and a 5Ω resistor to ground.

Determine the Norton equivalent between terminals X and Y for a circuit with a 15V source in series with a 10Ω resistor, paralleled with a 20Ω resistor, and then connected to a 5Ω resistor in series with a dependent current source of 0.2Vx (where Vx is the voltage across the 5Ω resistor).

Calculate the power dissipated by the 8Ω resistor in a circuit where a 24V source is connected to a series combination of a 6Ω resistor and a parallel network of a 12Ω resistor and a series combination of two 4Ω resistors, with the 8Ω resistor connected across the entire parallel network.

Find the voltage Vab in a bridge circuit with resistors R1=2Ω, R2=4Ω, R3=6Ω, R4=8Ω, and R5=10Ω, where a 20V source is connected across the bridge from the junction of R1 and R2 to the junction of R3 and R4, and Vab is measured between the junctions of R1-R3 and R2-R4.

Determine the current supplied by the 30V source in a circuit consisting of a delta network with resistors 9Ω, 12Ω, and 18Ω connected to a wye network with resistors 6Ω, 8Ω, and 10Ω, where the source is applied across one corner of the delta and the corresponding wye connection.

Calculate the equivalent capacitance between terminals A and B in an infinite ladder network where each section has a 4μF capacitor in series followed by a 6μF capacitor in parallel with the next section. Derive and solve the recurrence relation.

Find the time constant for the RC circuit where a 10V source charges a network of a 2kΩ resistor in series with a parallel combination of a 3kΩ resistor and a series combination of a 1kΩ resistor and a 4μF capacitor, with another 2μF capacitor in parallel with the entire combination.

Determine the steady-state voltage across the capacitor in an RLC circuit with R=50Ω, L=0.1H, C=100μF, driven by a voltage source v(t)=10cos(100t) V. Consider initial conditions zero and find the particular solution.
Calculate the resonant frequency and bandwidth for a parallel RLC circuit with R=1kΩ, L=10mH, and C=0.1μF. Also determine the quality factor Q and the impedance at resonance.

Find the current i(t) for t>0 in a circuit with a 20V source, a 5Ω resistor, and a 2H inductor in series, where the switch closes at t=0, and there's an initial current of 2A through the inductor. Consider the source is DC.

Determine the Thevenin impedance between terminals A and B at ω=1000 rad/s for a circuit containing a 10mH inductor in series with a parallel combination of a 100Ω resistor and a 10μF capacitor, with another 50Ω resistor in series with the parallel network.

Calculate the average power delivered by the source in an AC circuit with v(t)=100cos(377t) V connected to a load consisting of a 10Ω resistor in series with a 50mH inductor, paralleled with a 20μF capacitor.

Find the transfer function H(s)=Vout(s)/Vin(s) for a circuit with an op-amp configured as an inverting amplifier with R1=1kΩ, R2=2kΩ, and a feedback capacitor C=0.1μF in parallel with R2. Assume ideal op-amp.
Determine the output voltage vo(t) for an op-amp integrator circuit with R=10kΩ, C=1μF, and input voltage vin(t)=2sin(100t) V. Assume ideal op-amp and zero initial conditions.

Calculate the diode current in a circuit with a 5V source, a 1kΩ resistor, and a silicon diode (with forward voltage drop 0.7V) in series, and find the voltage across the resistor. Use the piecewise linear model.
Find the operating point (ICQ, VCEQ) for a BJT amplifier with VCC=12V, RB=200kΩ, RC=2kΩ, RE=1kΩ, and β=100. Assume VBE=0.7V and use the DC analysis model.
Determine the small-signal voltage gain Av=vo/vi for a common-emitter amplifier with RC=5kΩ, RE=500Ω, β=150, VA=∞, and ICQ=1mA. Assume thermal voltage VT=25mV and neglect Early effect.

A Carnot engine operates between two reservoirs at temperatures T_H = 600 K and T_C = 300 K. In each cycle, it absorbs 1000 J from the hot reservoir. (a) Calculate the work output per cycle and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir. (b) If the engine is run in reverse as a refrigerator between the same reservoirs, and 1000 J of work is supplied per cycle, calculate the heat extracted from the cold reservoir and the heat delivered to the hot reservoir. (c) Now consider a cascade system: the work output from the first Carnot engine is used to drive a second Carnot refrigerator between reservoirs at T_C = 300 K and an even colder reservoir at T_C' = 200 K. If the first engine still absorbs 1000 J from the 600 K reservoir, calculate the total heat extracted from the 200 K reservoir per cycle of the first engine.

One mole of an ideal monatomic gas undergoes a cyclic process consisting of three steps: (1) Isothermal expansion at T1 = 400 K from volume V1 to V2 = 2V1, (2) Isochoric cooling to temperature T2 = 300 K, (3) Adiabatic compression back to the initial state. (a) Sketch the cycle on a P-V diagram. (b) Calculate the net work done by the gas per cycle. (c) Calculate the efficiency of the cycle. (d) Compare this efficiency to that of a Carnot engine operating between the same maximum and minimum temperatures.

A building is maintained at 22°C using a heat pump with a COP of 3.5. On a day when the outside temperature is 0°C, the heat loss from the building is given by Q_loss = k(T_in - T_out), with k = 250 W/K. (a) Determine the power required to drive the heat pump. (b) If the heat pump is replaced by a Carnot heat pump operating between the same temperatures, what would be the required power? (c) Now suppose the heat pump is used in summer as an air conditioner (working in reverse) to maintain the building at 22°C when the outside temperature is 35°C. If the COP for cooling is 2.8, calculate the power required. (d) What is the maximum possible COP for cooling under these summer conditions?

A mixture of 2 kg of ice at -10°C and 3 kg of water at 30°C is placed in an insulated container. The specific heat of ice is 2100 J/kg·K, of water is 4186 J/kg·K, and the latent heat of fusion of ice is 334 kJ/kg. (a) Determine the final equilibrium temperature and phase composition of the mixture. (b) Calculate the total entropy change of the system. (c) Is this process reversible? Justify your answer.

A gas turbine operates on the Brayton cycle with air as the working fluid. The pressure ratio is 8, and the maximum and minimum temperatures are 1200 K and 300 K respectively. Assume constant specific heats: c_p = 1005 J/kg·K, c_v = 718 J/kg·K, γ = 1.4. (a) Calculate the temperatures at each state of the cycle. (b) Determine the net work output per kg of air. (c) Calculate the thermal efficiency. (d) How does this compare to the efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature extremes? (e) If a regenerator with effectiveness 0.75 is added, recalculate the thermal efficiency.

A vertical cylinder fitted with a frictionless piston contains 0.1 kg of saturated liquid water at 100 kPa. The water is heated slowly until its volume increases by a factor of 10. (a) Determine the final temperature and pressure if the process is isothermal. (b) Determine the final temperature and pressure if the process is isobaric. (c) For each case, calculate the work done and heat transferred. Use steam tables or appropriate property relations.

A combined gas-steam power plant has a gas turbine cycle with pressure ratio 10 and turbine inlet temperature 1400 K. The exhaust from the gas turbine enters a heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) that produces steam at 8 MPa and 500°C. The steam expands in a turbine to a condenser pressure of 10 kPa. Assume isentropic efficiencies of 85% for both gas and steam turbines. For air: c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4. (a) Determine the temperature of the gas turbine exhaust. (b) Calculate the mass flow rate ratio of steam to air if the HRSG has an effectiveness of 0.9. (c) Determine the overall efficiency of the combined cycle.

A rigid tank of volume 0.5 m³ contains helium at 200 kPa and 300 K. A valve is opened, and helium escapes slowly until the pressure drops to 100 kPa. During this process, heat is transferred to the helium to maintain its temperature constant. (a) Determine the mass of helium that escapes. (b) Calculate the heat transfer. (c) Determine the entropy generation, assuming the surroundings are at 300 K. (d) Is this process reversible? Explain.

A counterflow heat exchanger is used to cool oil (c_p = 2.1 kJ/kg·K) from 150°C to 50°C using water (c_p = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) that enters at 20°C and exits at 80°C. The oil flow rate is 0.5 kg/s. (a) Determine the water flow rate. (b) Calculate the log mean temperature difference (LMTD). (c) If the overall heat transfer coefficient is 500 W/m²·K, determine the required heat transfer area. (d) What is the effectiveness of the heat exchanger?

An ideal Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 9. At the beginning of compression, air is at 100 kPa and 300 K. The maximum cycle temperature is 1800 K. Assume constant specific heats: c_v = 0.718 kJ/kg·K, c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4. (a) Determine the temperature and pressure at each state of the cycle. (b) Calculate the net work output per kg of air. (c) Determine the thermal efficiency. (d) If the engine operates at 3000 rpm and has a displacement volume of 1.5 liters, calculate the power output for a 4-cylinder engine.

A mixture of 40% N₂ and 60% CO₂ by volume is contained in a rigid tank at 500 kPa and 300 K. The mixture is heated until the pressure doubles. (a) Determine the final temperature. (b) Calculate the heat transfer per kg of mixture. (c) Determine the change in entropy per kg of mixture. Use: For N₂: c_v = 0.743 kJ/kg·K, M = 28 kg/kmol; For CO₂: c_v = 0.657 kJ/kg·K, M = 44 kg/kmol.

A reversible heat engine operates between three reservoirs at temperatures T1 = 1000 K, T2 = 500 K, and T3 = 300 K. The engine receives 1000 kJ from the reservoir at T1 and does work W. It rejects heat Q2 to the reservoir at T2 and Q3 to the reservoir at T3. (a) Determine the maximum work output. (b) Find the values of Q2 and Q3 for maximum work. (c) If the engine is operated irreversibly with an efficiency of 50% of the maximum possible efficiency, calculate the work output and the total entropy generation.

A well-insulated piston-cylinder device contains 0.05 kg of steam at 1 MPa and 300°C. The steam expands in a reversible adiabatic process until the pressure is 200 kPa. (a) Determine the final temperature and quality (if applicable). (b) Calculate the work done by the steam. (c) Now consider if the expansion is irreversible with an isentropic efficiency of 85%. Determine the final state and the actual work output.

A refrigeration cycle uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator pressure is 200 kPa and the condenser pressure is 800 kPa. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid and leaves the evaporator as saturated vapor. The compressor has an isentropic efficiency of 80%. (a) Determine the COP of the cycle. (b) If the refrigeration load is 5 kW, calculate the mass flow rate of refrigerant and the power input to the compressor. (c) Determine the rate of entropy generation in the compressor, assuming the surroundings are at 25°C.

A solar pond has a surface area of 5000 m² and receives an average solar radiation of 200 W/m². The pond absorbs 60% of this radiation. The bottom layer of the pond is maintained at 80°C while the top layer is at 30°C. A heat engine operates between these two layers. (a) Calculate the maximum possible power output from the heat engine. (b) If the actual engine has an efficiency of 40% of the Carnot efficiency, determine the actual power output. (c) What is the rate of entropy generation in the actual engine?

A gas mixture consists of 1 kg of oxygen and 2 kg of nitrogen at 300 K and 500 kPa. The mixture is compressed polytropically (PV^n = constant) to twice the initial pressure. The polytropic exponent n = 1.3. (a) Determine the final temperature and volume. (b) Calculate the work done and heat transferred during the process. (c) Determine the change in entropy of the mixture. Use: For O₂: c_v = 0.658 kJ/kg·K, M = 32 kg/kmol; For N₂: c_v = 0.743 kJ/kg·K, M = 28 kg/kmol.

A Rankine cycle operates with steam as the working fluid. The boiler pressure is 8 MPa, the condenser pressure is 10 kPa, and the turbine inlet temperature is 500°C. The turbine has an isentropic efficiency of 90% and the pump has an isentropic efficiency of 85%. (a) Determine the thermal efficiency of the cycle. (b) Calculate the mass flow rate required for a net power output of 50 MW. (c) If the boiler is heated by hot gases that cool from 1200 K to 600 K, determine the mass flow rate of gases required, assuming c_p = 1.1 kJ/kg·K for the gases.

A house is heated by a geothermal heat pump that extracts heat from the ground at 10°C. The house is maintained at 22°C and has a heat loss rate of 15 kW. The heat pump has a COP of 3.2. (a) Determine the power input to the heat pump. (b) Calculate the rate of heat extraction from the ground. (c) If the heat pump operates for 2000 hours per year and electricity costs $0.12 per kWh, determine the annual heating cost. (d) What would be the annual cost if instead electric resistance heating were used?

A two-stage compression refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at -20°C, the condenser at 800 kPa, and there is intercooling with flash chamber at 400 kPa. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid and leaves the evaporator as saturated vapor. Both compressors have isentropic efficiencies of 85%. (a) Determine the COP of the system. (b) Calculate the power input for a refrigeration load of 10 kW. (c) Compare this with the COP and power input of a single-stage system operating between the same pressures.

An ideal Diesel cycle has a compression ratio of 18 and a cutoff ratio of 2. At the beginning of compression, air is at 95 kPa and 300 K. Assume constant specific heats: c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, c_v = 0.718 kJ/kg·K, γ = 1.4. (a) Determine the temperature and pressure at each state of the cycle. (b) Calculate the net work output per kg of air. (c) Determine the thermal efficiency. (d) Calculate the mean effective pressure.

A spacecraft is in a circular low Earth orbit (LEO) at an altitude of 400 km. It needs to transfer to a geostationary orbit (GEO) at 35,786 km altitude. Calculate the total delta-v required using a Hohmann transfer, considering Earth's gravitational parameter μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s². Then, determine the time of flight for the transfer orbit.

A satellite is in an elliptical orbit around Earth with perigee altitude of 500 km and apogee altitude of 20,000 km. Calculate the orbital period, eccentricity, and the velocity at perigee and apogee. Given Earth's radius = 6,378 km and μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 300 km altitude. It performs a single impulsive burn to increase its velocity by 1 km/s. Determine the new orbit's apogee altitude, eccentricity, and orbital period. Earth radius = 6,378 km, μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

Two satellites are in circular orbits around Earth: Satellite A at 500 km altitude and Satellite B at 800 km altitude. Calculate the relative angular velocity between them and determine after how many orbits of Satellite A they will realign (return to the same relative position). Earth radius = 6,378 km, μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 400 km altitude. It needs to rendezvous with a target in the same circular orbit but 30° ahead. Design a two-impulse rendezvous maneuver using a phasing orbit with a period difference. Calculate the required delta-v for both burns and the time until rendezvous. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A satellite is in a geostationary orbit (altitude 35,786 km). Calculate the delta-v required to transfer to a circular orbit at the Moon's average distance (384,400 km from Earth's center) using a Hohmann transfer. Also compute the time of flight. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

An interplanetary spacecraft is on a Hohmann transfer from Earth to Mars. Given Earth's orbital radius = 1.496×10^8 km, Mars' orbital radius = 2.279×10^8 km, Sun's μ = 1.327×10^11 km³/s², calculate the transfer orbit's semi-major axis, eccentricity, time of flight, and the required departure delta-v from Earth's orbit (assuming circular orbits).

A satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee radius 7,000 km and apogee radius 10,000 km. At apogee, a burn is performed to circularize the orbit at the apogee radius. Calculate the required delta-v and the new orbital period. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 600 km altitude. It needs to change its orbital plane by 10° while keeping the same circular altitude. Calculate the required delta-v for a combined plane change and circular orbit maintenance. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A satellite is in a circular orbit at 500 km altitude. A malfunction causes it to lose 0.5 km/s of velocity instantaneously. Determine the new orbit's parameters: perigee altitude, apogee altitude, eccentricity, and whether it will re-enter Earth's atmosphere (assume atmosphere starts at 100 km). μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

Two spacecraft are in the same circular orbit at 400 km altitude, separated by 180°. Design a rendezvous maneuver using a two-impulse Hohmann transfer that minimizes delta-v. Calculate the required burns and the time until rendezvous. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit around Earth at 1,000 km altitude. Calculate the delta-v required to escape Earth's gravitational influence (reach parabolic trajectory) and the additional delta-v needed to achieve a hyperbolic excess velocity of 3 km/s. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee altitude 200 km and apogee altitude 5,000 km. Calculate the true anomaly at which the satellite's speed equals the circular orbit speed at that radius. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 800 km altitude. It performs a burn to increase its velocity, resulting in a new orbit with perigee at 800 km and apogee at 12,000 km altitude. Calculate the delta-v of the burn and the new orbital period. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A satellite is in a geostationary orbit. Calculate the delta-v required to transfer to a Molniya orbit (12-hour period, eccentricity ≈ 0.72, inclination = 63.4°) with the same semi-major axis. Consider both the plane change and eccentricity change. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 500 km altitude. It needs to transfer to a co-elliptical orbit with perigee at 500 km and apogee at 1,500 km altitude, then rendezvous with a target at apogee. Calculate the delta-v for the transfer burn and the waiting time until the target reaches apogee if it's currently at perigee. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

An Earth observation satellite is in a Sun-synchronous orbit at 600 km altitude with an inclination that provides a repeating ground track every 15 orbits. Calculate the required inclination and the orbital period. Earth radius = 6,378 km, μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², J₂ = 1.0826×10⁻³.

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 300 km altitude. Calculate the delta-v required for a bi-elliptic transfer to a circular orbit at 1,000 km altitude, with an intermediate apogee at 2,000 km altitude. Compare this with the delta-v for a direct Hohmann transfer. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

A satellite is in an elliptical orbit with perigee radius 6,600 km and apogee radius 10,000 km. Calculate the delta-v required at perigee to raise the apogee to 15,000 km while keeping perigee unchanged. Also compute the new orbital period. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s².

A spacecraft is in a circular orbit at 400 km altitude. It needs to perform a plane change of 5° and simultaneously increase its altitude to 800 km circular orbit. Calculate the optimal delta-v for this combined maneuver compared to performing the maneuvers separately. μ = 3.986×10^5 km³/s², Earth radius = 6,378 km.

In pea plants, flower color is determined by a single gene with two alleles: P (purple, dominant) and p (white, recessive). Additionally, plant height is determined by a separate gene with two alleles: T (tall, dominant) and t (short, recessive). A heterozygous purple, homozygous tall plant is crossed with a white, heterozygous tall plant. What is the probability that an offspring will be purple and tall? Show your reasoning step by step.

In rabbits, coat color is controlled by a gene with three alleles in a dominance series: C (full color, dominant to all), c^ch (chinchilla, dominant to c^h and c), c^h (Himalayan, dominant to c), and c (albino, recessive to all). A chinchilla rabbit (genotype c^ch c) is crossed with a Himalayan rabbit (genotype c^h c). What are the possible phenotypes of their offspring and the probability of each?

In humans, ABO blood type is determined by three alleles: I^A, I^B, and i. I^A and I^B are codominant, and both are dominant to i. A woman with type A blood (whose father was type O) marries a man with type B blood (whose mother was type O). What are the possible blood types of their children and the probability of each?

In Drosophila, eye color is sex-linked. The gene is located on the X chromosome, with R (red eyes, dominant) and r (white eyes, recessive). A white-eyed female is crossed with a red-eyed male. What are the phenotypes and genotypes of the F1 generation? If F1 siblings are crossed, what are the phenotypes and genotypes of the F2 generation?

In snapdragons, flower color exhibits incomplete dominance: RR (red), Rr (pink), rr (white). A pink-flowered plant is crossed with a white-flowered plant. The offspring are then allowed to self-pollinate. What is the probability that a plant from the F2 generation will be pink?

In cats, coat color is determined by a sex-linked gene on the X chromosome: X^B (black), X^b (orange), and X^B X^b (calico/tortoiseshell due to X-inactivation in females). A calico female is crossed with an orange male. What are the possible phenotypes of their offspring and the probability of each?

In corn, kernel color is controlled by two genes that exhibit epistasis. Gene A (alleles A, a) controls pigment production: A_ allows pigment, aa results in no pigment (white). Gene B (alleles B, b) controls pigment type: B_ results in purple, bb results in red. A plant heterozygous for both genes is self-crossed. What is the phenotypic ratio of the offspring?

In humans, cystic fibrosis is caused by a recessive allele (c) on an autosome. A couple, both carriers (Cc), have three children. What is the probability that exactly two of the three children have cystic fibrosis?

In peas, seed shape is determined by a gene with two alleles: R (round, dominant) and r (wrinkled, recessive). A heterozygous round-seeded plant is crossed with a wrinkled-seeded plant. If three offspring are produced, what is the probability that at least one has wrinkled seeds?

In rabbits, fur length is determined by a gene with two alleles: L (long, dominant) and l (short, recessive). A breeder has a long-haired rabbit of unknown genotype. To determine its genotype, she performs a test cross with a short-haired rabbit. If the rabbit is heterozygous, what is the probability that among four offspring, exactly three will have long hair?

In chickens, comb shape is determined by two genes that exhibit epistasis: Gene P (alleles P, p) and Gene R (alleles R, r). The genotypes P_ R_ result in walnut comb, P_ rr result in pea comb, pp R_ result in rose comb, and pp rr result in single comb. A walnut-combed chicken (heterozygous for both genes) is crossed with a single-combed chicken. What are the possible comb shapes of the offspring and their probabilities?

In humans, Huntington's disease is caused by a dominant allele (H). A man with Huntington's disease (heterozygous) marries a woman without the disease. They have four children. What is the probability that at least two of the children inherit Huntington's disease?

In mice, coat color is determined by two genes: Gene B (alleles B, b) for color (B_ = black, bb = brown) and Gene C (alleles C, c) for pigment deposition (C_ = color, cc = albino). A black mouse (heterozygous for both genes) is crossed with an albino mouse (genotype bb cc). What are the phenotypic ratios of the offspring?

In plants, flower position is determined by a gene with two alleles: A (axial, dominant) and a (terminal, recessive). A heterozygous axial plant is self-pollinated. From the offspring, two plants are chosen at random and crossed. What is the probability that this cross produces only axial-flowered offspring?

In Drosophila, wing shape is determined by a gene with two alleles: V (vestigial wings, recessive) and V+ (normal wings, dominant). Body color is determined by a separate gene with two alleles: E (ebony body, recessive) and E+ (normal body, dominant). A fly heterozygous for both genes is test-crossed. What proportion of the offspring are expected to have normal wings and normal body color?

In humans, albinism is caused by a recessive allele (a) on an autosome. A normally pigmented man and woman, both of whom have one albino parent, have fraternal twins. What is the probability that both twins are normally pigmented?

In cattle, coat color is determined by a gene with codominant alleles: R (red), W (white), and RW (roan, a mixture of red and white hairs). A roan bull is crossed with a white cow. They produce two offspring. What is the probability that one is roan and the other is white?

In peas, pod color is determined by a gene with two alleles: G (green, dominant) and g (yellow, recessive). A green-podded plant of unknown genotype is self-pollinated. Among its offspring, 75% have green pods and 25% have yellow pods. What was the genotype of the original plant? Show the cross that leads to this outcome.

In humans, hemophilia is a sex-linked recessive disorder (allele h). A woman who is a carrier for hemophilia marries a man with hemophilia. They have two children. What is the probability that both children are boys with hemophilia?

In corn, kernel texture is determined by a gene with two alleles: S (smooth, dominant) and s (wrinkled, recessive). A farmer crosses two smooth-kerneled plants and obtains 200 offspring, of which 150 are smooth and 50 are wrinkled. What are the most likely genotypes of the parent plants? Show the cross that produces this ratio.

Given sequences S1 = 'AGCT' and S2 = 'AGC', compute the optimal global alignment score using the Needleman-Wunsch algorithm with match score = +2, mismatch penalty = -1, and gap penalty = -2. Show the complete dynamic programming matrix and traceback to find the aligned sequences.

Align sequences S1 = 'TACGGTA' and S2 = 'TAGCTA' using the Smith-Waterman algorithm for local alignment with match = +3, mismatch = -2, gap = -1. Determine the highest-scoring local alignment and its score, showing the DP matrix and traceback path.

Given three sequences: SeqA = 'ATCG', SeqB = 'ACGG', SeqC = 'ATGC', perform a progressive multiple sequence alignment using the guide tree (SeqA, (SeqB, SeqC)). Use match=+1, mismatch=-1, gap=-2 for pairwise alignments. Show the final multiple alignment and explain each step.

For sequences S1 = 'GGATC' and S2 = 'GAATTC', compute the optimal alignment allowing affine gap penalties: gap opening = -4, gap extension = -1, match = +2, mismatch = -1. Build the three-state DP matrices (M, Ix, Iy) and find the best alignment.

Align S1 = 'CAGTC' and S2 = 'CAGTGC' using the Gotoh algorithm for affine gaps with parameters: match=+3, mismatch=-2, gap_open=-5, gap_extend=-1. Show the matrices and traceback to obtain the aligned sequences.

Given a profile HMM with three match states for a motif 'ATG', and sequences S1 = 'CATG', S2 = 'ATGC', S3 = 'AATG', compute the Viterbi path and probability for each sequence aligning to the HMM. Assume simple emission probabilities (match: 0.9 for correct nucleotide, 0.033 for others; insert: uniform 0.25).

Perform a global alignment of S1 = 'TAGACA' and S2 = 'TAA' using the Hirschberg algorithm to save space. Use scores: match=+1, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. Show the recursive division steps and final alignment.
Given S1 = 'ACGTACGT' and S2 = 'ACGT', find all local alignments with score ≥ +6 using the Smith-Waterman method with match=+2, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. List each alignment and its score.

Align S1 = 'GATTACA' and S2 = 'GCATGCU' considering RNA nucleotides (including U). Use global alignment with match=+2, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. Account for U pairing with A as a match. Show the DP matrix and alignment.

Compute the optimal semi-global alignment (where gaps at ends are free) for S1 = 'AAAGGG' and S2 = 'TTAGGG' with match=+1, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. Determine the best alignment and score, explaining how end-gap penalties are handled.
Given a scoring matrix where transitions (A↔G, C↔T) are penalized less (-1) than transversions (-2), and match=+3, gap=-2, align S1 = 'ACGT' and S2 = 'AGCT'. Show the custom DP matrix and alignment.

For sequences S1 = 'ATAT' and S2 = 'TATA', compute the optimal alignment using a linear gap penalty of -3 and a match score of +2. Then, recompute with an affine gap penalty (open=-4, extend=-1). Compare the alignments and scores.

Perform a multiple sequence alignment of Seq1 = 'HEAG', Seq2 = 'HEA', Seq3 = 'HE' using the sum-of-pairs objective with match=+1, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. Show the progressive alignment steps and final MSA.

Given S1 = 'ACGT' and S2 = 'ACGT', but with a constraint that positions 2 and 3 must be aligned together if possible, incorporate this into the DP algorithm. Use match=+2, mismatch=-1, gap=-3. Show the constrained DP matrix and alignment.

Align S1 = 'AAAA' and S2 = 'TTTT' using a substitution matrix where like pairs (A-A, T-T, etc.) score +1, unlike pairs score -1, and gap=-2. Then, use a matrix where complementary pairs (A-T, C-G) score +2. Compare results.
Compute the edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between S1 = 'sunday' and S2 = 'saturday' using operations: insert=1, delete=1, substitute=2. Show the DP matrix and minimal edit sequence.

Given S1 = 'CAT' and S2 = 'ACT', use the Needleman-Wunsch algorithm with match=+1, mismatch=-1, gap=-2. Then, modify to allow free gaps at the start of S1 and end of S2. Compute the new optimal alignment and score.

For sequences S1 = 'GTAC' and S2 = 'GTC', perform global alignment with a band of width 2 (allowing at most 2 gaps difference). Use match=+2, mismatch=-1, gap=-3. Show the banded DP matrix and alignment.
Align S1 = 'MGH' and S2 = 'MGNH' where 'N' represents any nucleotide (wildcard). Use scores: match=+2, mismatch=-1, wildcard match=+1, gap=-2. Show the DP matrix and optimal alignment.

Given a phylogenetic tree with sequences: Leaf1 = 'AC', Leaf2 = 'AG', Leaf3 = 'GC', reconstruct ancestral sequences at internal nodes using Fitch's algorithm for parsimony. Then, compute the multiple alignment implied by the ancestral states.

Name the compound with the following structure: A cyclohexane ring with a methyl group at position 1, an ethyl group at position 3, and a chlorine atom at position 4. The methyl group at position 1 is on the same side as the chlorine at position 4 when viewed in the chair conformation. Provide the full IUPAC name including stereochemistry.

Name the compound: A benzene ring with a nitro group at position 1, a bromine at position 3, and a carboxylic acid group at position 4. Additionally, there is a propyl chain attached to position 2 of the benzene ring.

Name the compound: A six-carbon chain with a double bond between carbons 2 and 3, and a triple bond between carbons 4 and 5. There is a chlorine atom attached to carbon 2 and a hydroxyl group attached to carbon 6. Provide E/Z designation for the double bond.

Name the compound: A cyclopentane ring with two methyl groups: one at position 1 and one at position 3. Both methyl groups are on the same side of the ring. Also, there is an aldehyde group attached to carbon 2.

Name the compound: A seven-carbon chain with a ketone group at carbon 4. There is a double bond between carbons 2 and 3 with the higher priority groups on opposite sides. There is also a fluorine atom attached to carbon 6.

Name the compound: A bicyclic system consisting of a cyclohexane ring fused to a cyclopentane ring (decahydronaphthalene skeleton). There is a methyl group at the bridgehead carbon where the rings fuse, and an alcohol group on the carbon adjacent to the bridgehead on the cyclohexane ring.

Name the compound: A benzene ring with an amino group at position 1 and a methyl group at position 4. On carbon 2 of the benzene ring, there is a three-carbon chain with a double bond between the first and second carbons of the chain, and a carboxylic acid at the end.

Name the compound: A five-carbon chain with a bromine on carbon 1, a double bond between carbons 2 and 3 (with Z configuration), and a chlorine on carbon 4. Also, there is an ethyl group attached to carbon 3.
Name the compound: A cycloheptane ring with an isopropyl group at position 1 and a vinyl group at position 3. The isopropyl and vinyl groups are trans to each other across the ring.

Name the compound: A four-carbon chain with an aldehyde at carbon 1, a double bond between carbons 2 and 3 (E configuration), and a hydroxyl group on carbon 4. Additionally, there is a methyl branch on carbon 3.
Name the compound: A naphthalene ring system with a chlorine atom at position 2 and a nitro group at position 6. On carbon 8, there is a two-carbon chain ending in a carboxylic acid.

Name the compound: An eight-carbon chain with a triple bond between carbons 3 and 4, and a double bond between carbons 6 and 7 (with E configuration). There is a methyl group on carbon 5 and a bromine on carbon 8.

Name the compound: A cyclobutane ring with a carboxylic acid group attached to one carbon and an amino group attached to the adjacent carbon. The amino and carboxylic acid groups are cis to each other.
Name the compound: A benzene ring with a sulfonic acid group at position 1 and a methyl group at position 3. At position 4, there is a three-carbon chain with a ketone group at the second carbon.

Name the compound: A six-carbon chain with an alcohol on carbon 1, a double bond between carbons 3 and 4 (Z configuration), and a triple bond between carbons 4 and 5. Number the chain to give the lowest numbers to the multiple bonds.

Name the compound: A fused ring system of a benzene ring fused to a five-membered ring containing one oxygen (benzofuran skeleton). On the benzene portion, there is a methyl group at position 5, and on the furan ring, there is a bromine at position 2.

Name the compound: A five-carbon ring (cyclopentane) with a two-carbon chain (ethyl group) attached to carbon 1, and a one-carbon chain (methyl group) attached to carbon 3. Both substituents are on the same side of the ring. Also, there is a double bond between carbons 2 and 3 of the ring.
Name the compound: A seven-carbon chain with a ketone at carbon 2 and an alcohol at carbon 5. There is also a double bond between carbons 3 and 4 with E configuration, and a chlorine on carbon 6.
Name the compound: A benzene ring with an isopropyl group at position 1 and a carboxylic acid at position 3. At position 4, there is a two-carbon chain with an amino group at the end.

Name the compound: A six-membered ring with oxygen (pyran) with a double bond between carbons 3 and 4, and another double bond between carbons 5 and 6. There is a methyl group at carbon 2 and an aldehyde group at carbon 6.

White to move and win. White: Kc5, Ra1, pawns a2, b3. Black: Kc7, Rb8, pawns a6, b7. White's pawns are advanced but Black's rook is active. Find the winning sequence that promotes a pawn while neutralizing Black's counterplay.
White to move and draw. White: Kg2, pawn h2. Black: Kg4, pawn h3. Black's king is in front of the pawn. Find the defensive resource that holds the draw despite being a pawn down.
White to move and win. White: Ke5, pawn d6. Black: Ke8, pawn d7. Both kings are in front of their pawns. Calculate the precise king maneuvers to promote first or capture the opponent's pawn.
White to move and win. White: Kf1, Rg1, pawn h6. Black: Kh8, Rg8, pawn h7. Black is in a passive rook vs. pawn endgame. Find the winning breakthrough that forces promotion or wins the rook.
White to move and draw. White: Kb1, pawn a2. Black: Kc3, pawn a3. Black's king is ideally placed. Find the only move that draws by creating stalemate or perpetual threat.
White to move and win. White: Kg6, pawn f5. Black: Kg8, pawn f6. Both pawns are blocked. Determine the winning king triangulation or opposition maneuver to penetrate.
White to move and win. White: Kd4, pawns c3, e4. Black: Kd6, pawns c6, e5. Both sides have passed pawns. Calculate the sequence that wins a pawn or promotes first.
White to move and draw. White: Kf2, pawn g3. Black: Kf4, pawn g4. Black has the opposition. Find the defensive resource that holds the draw by creating a fortress or stalemate.
White to move and win. White: Kc2, pawns a3, b2. Black: Kc4, pawn a4. White has two connected passed pawns. Find the winning king move that supports promotion while stopping Black's pawn.
White to move and win. White: Ke3, pawn d3. Black: Ke5, pawn d4. Both pawns are on the same file. Calculate the precise king moves to win the pawn or promote.
White to move and draw. White: Kh1, pawn g2. Black: Kh3, pawn g3. Black's king is in front. Find the defensive idea that draws by stalemate or perpetual check threat.
White to move and win. White: Kf3, pawn g4. Black: Kf5, pawn g5. Both pawns are blocked. Determine the winning opposition or triangulation to break through.
White to move and win. White: Kb5, pawn a5. Black: Kb7, pawn a6. Both pawns are on the same file. Calculate the sequence that wins by zugzwang or pawn breakthrough.
White to move and draw. White: Kc1, pawn b2. Black: Kc3, pawn b3. Black's king is ideally placed. Find the only move that draws by stalemate or creating a perpetual threat.
White to move and win. White: Kg7, pawn h5. Black: Kg5, pawn h6. Both kings are active. Find the winning king maneuver to promote the pawn or capture Black's pawn.
White to move and draw. White: Kd1, pawn e2. Black: Kd3, pawn e3. Black has the opposition. Find the defensive resource that holds the draw by creating a fortress.
White to move and win. White: Kf6, pawn g5. Black: Kf4, pawn g6. Both pawns are blocked. Determine the winning king triangulation or opposition to penetrate.
White to move and win. White: Kc6, pawn b5. Black: Kc8, pawn b6. Both pawns are on the same file. Calculate the sequence that wins by zugzwang or pawn breakthrough.
White to move and draw. White: Ke1, pawn f2. Black: Ke3, pawn f3. Black's king is ideally placed. Find the only move that draws by stalemate or creating a perpetual threat.
White to move and win. White: Kh6, pawn g5. Black: Kh4, pawn g6. Both kings are active. Find the winning king maneuver to promote the pawn or capture Black's pawn.

Five friends (Alice, Bob, Charlie, Dana, and Eve) each have a different favorite color (red, blue, green, yellow, purple) and a different pet (dog, cat, bird, fish, hamster). Using the clues: 1) Alice's favorite color is not red or blue. 2) The person with the dog likes green. 3) Bob has either the cat or the bird. 4) Dana's favorite color is purple and she does not have a fish. 5) Eve does not like yellow and does not have a hamster. 6) The person who likes red has the fish. 7) Charlie's pet is not a dog or a cat. Determine each person's favorite color and pet.

At a conference, five researchers (Dr. Adams, Dr. Brown, Dr. Clark, Dr. Davis, Dr. Evans) presented papers on different days (Monday to Friday) in different rooms (101, 102, 103, 104, 105). Clues: 1) Dr. Adams presented two days before the presentation in room 104. 2) Dr. Brown presented in room 102, but not on Wednesday. 3) Dr. Clark presented on Friday, but not in room 105. 4) The presentation in room 101 was on Tuesday. 5) Dr. Davis presented the day after Dr. Evans. 6) Room 103 was used the day before room 105. Determine each researcher's day and room.

Five students (Anna, Ben, Chloe, David, Emma) took exams in five subjects (Math, Science, History, Art, Music) and got different grades (A, B, C, D, F). Clues: 1) Anna got a higher grade in Math than Ben did in Science. 2) Chloe got a B in either History or Art. 3) David's grade was not D or F, and his subject was not Music. 4) The student who got an A took History. 5) Emma's subject was not Science or Music, and her grade was not C. 6) Ben's grade was one letter better than the grade in Music. 7) The Art exam got a lower grade than the Science exam. Determine each student's subject and grade.

Five cars (a Honda, a Ford, a Toyota, a BMW, a Tesla) are parked in a row (positions 1 to 5 from left to right). Each is a different color (red, blue, green, white, black). Clues: 1) The Honda is somewhere to the left of the blue car. 2) The Tesla is at one end of the row. 3) The green car is immediately to the right of the white car. 4) The Ford is in position 3. 5) The red car is to the immediate left of the BMW. 6) The Toyota is not next to the Ford. Determine the position and color of each car.

Five people (Grace, Henry, Iris, Jack, Kelly) live in five houses (numbered 1 to 5) on a street. Each has a different profession (doctor, engineer, teacher, artist, chef) and a different favorite beverage (coffee, tea, juice, milk, water). Clues: 1) The doctor lives next to the artist. 2) Henry lives in house 3 and drinks tea. 3) The engineer lives in house 1. 4) Grace is the teacher and lives next to the coffee drinker. 5) The juice drinker lives in house 5. 6) Jack is the chef and does not live in house 2. 7) The milk drinker lives between the water drinker and the coffee drinker. Determine each person's house, profession, and beverage.

Five teams (Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Epsilon) competed in a tournament, finishing in positions 1st through 5th. Each team has a different captain (Alex, Blake, Casey, Drew, Ellis) and scored a different total points (25, 30, 35, 40, 45). Clues: 1) Alpha finished two places above the team with 35 points. 2) Blake's team scored 40 points. 3) Gamma finished immediately below Beta. 4) Casey's team finished 4th. 5) Delta did not finish last. 6) The team with 25 points finished immediately above Ellis's team. 7) Alex's team finished 1st. Determine each team's position, captain, and points.

Five books (Mystery, Fantasy, Sci-Fi, Romance, Biography) are on a shelf from top to bottom (positions 1 to 5). Each has a different author (A. Brown, C. Davis, E. Foster, G. Hill, I. Jones) and was published in a different year (2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022). Clues: 1) The Sci-Fi book is above the Mystery book. 2) E. Foster wrote the book published in 2020. 3) The Romance book is at position 2. 4) G. Hill's book is immediately below the Fantasy book. 5) The 2021 book is at position 4. 6) I. Jones wrote the Biography. 7) The 2019 book is above the book by A. Brown. Determine each book's position, genre, author, and year.

Five restaurants (Italian, Mexican, Chinese, Indian, French) were rated by five critics (P, Q, R, S, T) with different scores (1 to 5 stars). Clues: 1) The Italian restaurant got a higher rating than the Mexican. 2) Critic R gave a rating one star higher than critic S. 3) The Chinese restaurant got the lowest rating. 4) Critic P did not rate the Indian or French restaurants. 5) The French restaurant got a 4-star rating. 6) Critic Q rated the Mexican restaurant. 7) The Indian restaurant got a higher rating than the restaurant rated by critic T. Determine each restaurant's critic and rating.

Five musicians (violinist, pianist, guitarist, drummer, singer) performed in a concert in a specific order (1st to 5th). Each played a different genre (classical, jazz, rock, pop, blues). Clues: 1) The violinist performed immediately before the jazz performer. 2) The rock performer was 2nd. 3) The guitarist performed after the pianist but before the drummer. 4) The blues performer was last. 5) The pop performer was immediately after the classical performer. 6) The singer did not perform 1st or last. Determine the order, instrument, and genre for each performer.

Five countries (Australia, Brazil, Canada, Denmark, Egypt) participated in an event, each represented by a different athlete (Ava, Liam, Mia, Noah, Zoe). They finished in different places (1st to 5th) and earned different medal counts (0, 1, 2, 3, 4). Clues: 1) Australia finished better than Brazil but worse than Canada. 2) Liam represented the country with 3 medals. 3) Mia finished 4th. 4) The country with 0 medals finished last. 5) Denmark earned more medals than Egypt. 6) Noah represented Brazil. 7) Zoe finished immediately before Ava. Determine each country's athlete, place, and medal count.

Five movies (Action, Comedy, Drama, Horror, Thriller) were watched by five friends (Kai, Leo, Maya, Nina, Owen) on different days (Monday to Friday). Each movie has a different runtime (90, 100, 110, 120, 130 minutes). Clues: 1) Kai watched the movie on Tuesday. 2) The Comedy was 100 minutes long. 3) The Horror movie was watched the day after the Action movie. 4) Nina watched the Thriller. 5) The 130-minute movie was watched on Friday. 6) Leo watched a movie the day before Maya. 7) The Drama was 110 minutes and was not watched by Owen. Determine each friend's movie, day, and runtime.

Five cities (London, Paris, Tokyo, New York, Sydney) have different populations (5M, 8M, 10M, 12M, 15M) and are in different time zones (UTC, UTC+1, UTC+9, UTC-5, UTC+10). Clues: 1) Tokyo has a larger population than Paris but smaller than New York. 2) The city in UTC+10 has a population of 5M. 3) London is not in UTC or UTC+1. 4) The city with 12M people is in UTC-5. 5) Sydney is in UTC+10. 6) Paris is in UTC+1. 7) The city with 8M people is not in UTC+9. Determine each city's population and time zone.

Five fruits (Apple, Banana, Cherry, Date, Elderberry) were bought by five people (Sam, Taylor, Jordan, Casey, Riley) in different quantities (1, 2, 3, 4, 5). Each fruit has a different price per unit ($0.50, $1.00, $1.50, $2.00, $2.50). Clues: 1) Sam bought more units than Taylor. 2) The Banana cost $1.50 per unit. 3) Casey bought the Date. 4) The Elderberry cost $0.50 per unit and was bought in quantity 5. 5) Jordan bought 3 units of the fruit that costs $2.00. 6) The Apple was bought in quantity 2. 7) Riley spent a total of $5.00. Determine each person's fruit, quantity, and price per unit.

Five scientists (Biologist, Chemist, Physicist, Astronomer, Geologist) work in different labs (Lab A, B, C, D, E) and have different numbers of publications (10, 15, 20, 25, 30). Clues: 1) The Biologist works in Lab B. 2) The Chemist has 5 more publications than the Astronomer. 3) The Physicist works in Lab D. 4) The scientist in Lab A has 20 publications. 5) The Geologist has fewer publications than the Biologist. 6) Lab E has the scientist with 30 publications. 7) The Astronomer does not work in Lab C. Determine each scientist's lab and publication count.

Five paintings (Landscape, Portrait, Abstract, Still Life, Modern) were created in different centuries (16th, 17th, 18th, 19th, 20th) by different artists (Van Gogh, Monet, Rembrandt, Picasso, Da Vinci). Clues: 1) The Portrait was painted in the 17th century. 2) Van Gogh painted the Modern artwork. 3) The Abstract was painted after the Still Life but before the Landscape. 4) Picasso did not paint in the 19th century. 5) Da Vinci painted in the 16th century. 6) Monet painted the Landscape. 7) Rembrandt did not paint the Still Life. Determine each painting's genre, century, and artist.

Five companies (TechCorp, HealthInc, FinancePro, EduServe, RetailCo) have different employee counts (200, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000) and are located in different cities (Austin, Boston, Chicago, Denver, Seattle). Clues: 1) TechCorp has more employees than HealthInc but fewer than FinancePro. 2) The company in Seattle has 2000 employees. 3) EduServe is not in Boston or Chicago. 4) The company with 500 employees is in Denver. 5) RetailCo is in Austin. 6) HealthInc is in Boston. 7) The company with 1000 employees is not in Chicago. Determine each company's employee count and city.

Five planets (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter) have different diameters (3000, 7500, 8000, 42000, 88000 miles) and different numbers of moons (0, 1, 2, 4, 79). Clues: 1) Venus has a larger diameter than Earth but smaller than Jupiter. 2) The planet with 79 moons has a diameter of 88000 miles. 3) Mercury has 0 moons. 4) Mars has more moons than Earth. 5) The planet with diameter 42000 miles has 4 moons. 6) Earth has a diameter of 8000 miles. Determine each planet's diameter and moon count.

Five languages (Spanish, French, Mandarin, Arabic, Russian) are spoken by five diplomats (Mr. A, Mr. B, Mr. C, Mr. D, Mr. E) who come from different countries (Spain, France, China, Saudi Arabia, Russia). Each diplomat speaks exactly two languages, one of which is their native language. Clues: 1) Mr. A speaks Spanish and French. 2) The diplomat from China speaks Mandarin and Russian. 3) Mr. B does not speak Arabic. 4) The diplomat from Russia speaks Russian and French. 5) Mr. C speaks Arabic and Spanish. 6) Mr. D is from Saudi Arabia. 7) Mr. E speaks Mandarin and Arabic. Determine each diplomat's country and both languages spoken.

Five puzzles (Crossword, Sudoku, Jigsaw, Riddle, Maze) were solved by five people (Alex, Bailey, Casey, Drew, Ellis) in different times (5, 10, 15, 20, 25 minutes). Determine each flower's month and petal count.
